title: Actinomycosis
authors: Thomas A. Russo, John C. Hu

items.title: Recognize Actinomycosis Mimicking Malignancy
items.content: Actinomycosis can present with chronicity, progression across tissue boundaries, and mass-like features that mimic malignancy. This presentation often leads to misdiagnosis.
keyPoints: Classical presentations include chronicity, progression across tissue boundaries, and mass-like features.
Mimics malignancy, leading to frequent misdiagnosis.
Most commonly caused by A. israelii among other species.

items.title: Understand Polymicrobial Nature and Taxonomic Changes in Actinomycosis
items.content: Most actinomycotic infections are polymicrobial, involving various bacteria depending on the infection site. Recent genome-based analysis has expanded the list of implicated species and reclassified some to new genera.
keyPoints: Infections are often polymicrobial with organisms like Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans and Eikenella corrodens.
Genome-based analysis has led to taxonomic changes, expanding the list of implicated species.
33 species remain in the Actinomyces genus, with at least 26 causing human disease.

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology of Actinomycosis: Risk Factors and Trends
items.content: Actinomycosis is a globally distributed infection with no specific geographic boundaries, affecting individuals across all age groups but peaking in middle decades. Males are more frequently affected than females by a factor of three, potentially due to differences in dental hygiene practices or higher rates of trauma. The incidence has declined since the introduction of antibiotics, likely because of improved dental hygiene and early antimicrobial intervention. Certain populations, such as those with limited healthcare access, prolonged use of intrauterine contraceptive devices (IUDs), or treatment with bisphosphonates, are at increased risk.
keyPoints: Actinomycosis occurs worldwide across all age groups, peaking in middle decades.
Males have a three-fold higher incidence than females.
Decrease in incidence attributed to improved dental hygiene and early antibiotic use.
Higher risk for individuals with limited healthcare access, prolonged IUD use, or bisphosphonate treatment.

items.title: Pathogenesis of Actinomycosis: Key Mechanisms and Clinical Features
items.content: Actinomycosis is caused by bacteria from the normal oral flora, which can also be found in the bronchi, gastrointestinal tract, and female genital tract. The infection begins with a disruption of the mucosal barrier, leading to local infection that spreads slowly and contiguously across tissue planes. Actinomycosis is characterized by chronic indolent lesions with central necrosis containing neutrophils and sulfur granules, which are virtually diagnostic. The fibrotic mass walls are described as 'wooden.' Sinus tracts may develop over time, while hematogenous spread is rare. Foreign bodies, such as IUDs, can facilitate infection, and certain conditions or treatments (e.g., HIV, immunodeficiencies, anti-TNF agents) increase susceptibility.
keyPoints: Etiologic agents are part of normal oral flora, found in bronchi, GI tract, and female genital tract.
Disruption of mucosal barrier is critical for infection development.
Characteristic chronic lesions with central necrosis and sulfur granules.
Fibrotic mass walls described as 'wooden.'
Sinus tracts may develop; hematogenous spread is rare.
Foreign bodies, like IUDs, facilitate infection.
Increased risk in immunocompromised states or certain treatments.

items.title: Actinomycosis Mimics Neoplasms in Oral-Cervicofacial Disease
items.content: Oral-cervicofacial actinomycosis often presents as a soft tissue swelling, abscess, mass, or ulcerative lesion that can be mistaken for neoplasm. Dental diseases or procedures are common triggers, and the angle of the jaw is frequently involved.
keyPoints: Actinomycosis should be considered in any head and neck mass lesion or relapsing infection.
Radiation therapy and medication-related osteonecrosis (MRONJ) due to bisphosphonates and anti-RANKL agents increase actinomycotic infections of the mandible and maxilla.

items.title: Thoracic Actinomycosis Can Be Confused with Neoplasms or Pneumonia
items.content: Thoracic actinomycosis often follows an indolent course, involving the pulmonary parenchyma and/or pleural space. It is commonly mistaken for neoplasm or pneumonia due to its radiographic presentation.
keyPoints: Chest pain, fever, weight loss are common symptoms; cough may be productive.
Radiographic findings include mass lesions or pneumonia with central low attenuation and ring-like rim enhancement on CT.
>50% of cases involve pleural thickening, effusion, or empyema.

items.title: Pelvic Actinomycosis Often Associated with IUD Use
items.content: Actinomycotic involvement of the pelvis is frequently linked to intrauterine device (IUD) use, presenting indolently with symptoms like fever and abnormal vaginal bleeding. Diagnosis can be delayed, leading to misdiagnosis as malignancy.
keyPoints: Pelvic actinomycosis should be considered when pelvic symptoms occur in the context of IUD use or history.
The risk increases with prolonged IUD placement (>1 year).
Symptoms include indolent fever, weight loss, abdominal pain, and abnormal vaginal bleeding or discharge.

items.title: Management of Actinomycosis in Asymptomatic Patients with IUD
items.content: In asymptomatic patients with actinomyces-like organisms (ALOs) and an intrauterine device (IUD), education and close follow-up are recommended. Removal of the IUD is not necessary unless a suitable contraceptive alternative is available. If symptoms arise that cannot be explained, removing the IUD and initiating empirical treatment for possible early endometritis may be prudent if advanced disease is excluded.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic patients with ALOs do not require immediate IUD removal.
Close follow-up and patient education are essential.
IUD removal should be considered only when a suitable contraceptive alternative exists.
If unexplained symptoms occur, remove the IUD and consider 14-day empirical treatment for early endometritis if advanced disease is excluded.

items.title: Characteristics of Central Nervous System Actinomycosis
items.content: Actinomycosis of the central nervous system (CNS) is rare and often presents as single or multiple brain abscesses. Individuals with hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia are at increased risk. On CT, an abscess typically appears as a ring-enhancing lesion with a thick wall that may be irregular or nodular. Other manifestations include primary meningitis, epidural/subdural infections, and cavernous sinus syndrome.
keyPoints: CNS actinomycosis is rare but can present as brain abscesses.
Increased risk in individuals with hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia.
CT imaging shows ring-enhancing lesions with thick, irregular or nodular walls.
Possible CNS manifestations include primary meningitis and epidural/subdural infections.

items.title: Diagnosing Actinomycosis: Minimally Invasive Approaches
items.content: Actinomycosis diagnosis is often overlooked until post-surgery, but it can frequently be diagnosed with less invasive methods. Clinicians should consider actinomycosis early to avoid unnecessary surgery and utilize FDG-PET for detecting hypermetabolism associated with the disease.
keyPoints: FDG-PET demonstrates hypermetabolism in actinomycotic disease.
Aspirations and biopsies (with or without CT/ultrasound guidance) can be used to obtain diagnostic material.
Avoidance of antibiotics prior to testing is crucial for microbiologic identification.

items.title: Identifying Actinomycosis: Sulfur Granules and Microbiologic Techniques
items.content: The presence of sulfur granules in pus or tissues, identified microscopically, is key to diagnosing actinomycosis. Differentiation from similar conditions like mycetoma and botryomycosis requires histopathologic and microbiologic studies.
keyPoints: Sulfur granules are a defining characteristic of actinomycosis but can also be found in mycetoma and botryomycosis.
Microbiologic identification is optimized under anaerobic conditions, requiring 5–7 days up to 2–4 weeks for isolation.
16S rRNA gene amplification and MALDI-TOF MS are enhancing diagnostic sensitivity and specificity.

items.title: Treatment Guidelines for Actinomycosis
items.content: Actinomycosis requires prolonged treatment with high doses of antimicrobial agents due to poor drug penetration into thick-walled masses and biofilm-like sulfur granules. The standard regimen involves intravenous penicillin followed by oral therapy, lasting 6–12 months for serious infections.
keyPoints: IV administration of 18–24 million units of penicillin daily for 2–6 weeks
Followed by oral therapy with penicillin or amoxicillin for a total duration of 6–12 months
Alternatives for penicillin-allergic patients include tetracyclines, ceftriaxone, or carbapenems
CT and MRI are recommended to monitor treatment response and minimize relapse risk

items.title: Role of Imaging and Surgical Intervention in Actinomycosis Management
items.content: Imaging with CT and MRI is crucial for monitoring therapy response in actinomycosis. Medical therapy alone can be effective, but surgical intervention may be necessary for critical locations or treatment failure.
keyPoints: CT and MRI are most sensitive techniques to monitor therapy and minimize relapse risk
Medical therapy alone is increasingly supported even in extensive disease
Surgical intervention considered when medical therapy fails or critical locations are involved (e.g., CNS, epidural space)
Percutaneous drainage for well-defined abscesses combined with medical therapy

items.question: Which genus is primarily responsible for causing classical actinomycosis?
items.explanation: Classical actinomycosis is primarily caused by bacteria of the genus Actinomyces.

items.question: Which of the following species has been increasingly recognized as a cause of actinomycotic infections?
items.explanation: Infections due to Winkia (Actinomyces) neuii have been increasingly recognized.

items.question: How many species remain in the Actinomyces genus that are implicated as causes of human disease?
items.explanation: At present, 33 species remain in the Actinomyces genus with at least 26 species implicated as causes of human disease.

items.question: Which factor is associated with a higher incidence of actinomycosis in males compared to females?
options: Poorer dental hygiene and/or more frequent trauma
Increased use of intrauterine contraceptive devices (IUDs)
More common occurrence of chronic granulomatous disease
items.explanation: Males have a three-fold higher incidence, possibly due to poorer dental hygiene and/or more frequent trauma.

items.question: What is the critical step in the development of actinomycosis?
items.explanation: The critical step in actinomycosis development is disruption of the mucosal barrier.

items.question: Which feature is virtually diagnostic for actinomycosis?
options: Acute inflammation at the infection site
Central necrosis consisting of neutrophils and sulfur granules
items.explanation: Central necrosis with neutrophils and sulfur granules is virtually diagnostic for actinomycosis.

items.question: Which site is most frequently involved in oral-cervicofacial actinomycosis?
items.explanation: The angle of the jaw is generally involved in oral-cervicofacial actinomycosis.

items.question: What condition has been recognized as contributing to an increasing incidence of actinomycotic infection of the mandible and maxilla?
items.explanation: Radiation therapy, MRONJ due to antiresorptive therapy with bisphosphonates and anti-RANKL agents like denosumab, angiogenesis inhibitors, and tyrosine kinase inhibitors have all been recognized as contributing factors.

items.question: What is a common radiographic finding in thoracic actinomycosis?
items.explanation: The usual radiographic findings are either a mass lesion or pneumonia, with central areas of low attenuation and ring-like rim enhancement seen on CT.

items.question: Which symptom is uncommon in pelvic actinomycosis?
items.explanation: Pelvic actinomycosis only uncommonly includes ascites and lymphadenopathy.

items.question: What is the recommended management for asymptomatic patients with ALOs and an IUD?
options: Initiation of a 14-day course of empirical treatment
Education, close follow-up, and consideration of alternative contraception if necessary
items.explanation: Asymptomatic patients with ALOs should receive education and close follow-up without removing the IUD unless a suitable contraceptive alternative is agreed upon.

items.question: Which imaging finding is most characteristic of an Actinomyces brain abscess on CT?
options: Ring-enhancing lesion with a thick, possibly irregular or nodular wall
items.explanation: An Actinomyces brain abscess typically appears on CT as a ring-enhancing lesion with a thick wall that may be irregular or nodular.

items.question: Which organism is most commonly involved in disseminated disease of Actinomycosis?
items.explanation: In disseminated disease of Actinomycosis, S. meyeri is most commonly involved.

items.question: Which imaging modality has demonstrated hypermetabolism in actinomycotic disease?
items.explanation: Hypermetabolism has been demonstrated by $^{18}$F-FDG PET in actinomycotic disease.

items.question: What is the most common means of diagnosing actinomycosis?
options: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS)
items.explanation: The microscopic identification of sulfur granules is the most common means of diagnosing actinomycosis.

items.question: What can preclude microbiologic identification of actinomycetes?
items.explanation: Microbiologic identification of actinomycetes is often precluded by prior antimicrobial therapy.

items.question: What is the recommended initial intravenous treatment regimen for serious actinomycosis infections?
options: 18–24 million units of penicillin daily for 2–6 weeks, followed by oral therapy.
Tetracyclines or ceftriaxone for 4–8 weeks without follow-up oral therapy.
Carbapenems alone for a total duration of 3 months.
Azithromycin as the first-line treatment due to its in vitro efficacy.
items.explanation: The recommended initial IV regimen is 18–24 million units of penicillin daily for 2–6 weeks, followed by oral therapy with penicillin or amoxicillin.

items.question: Which imaging techniques are most sensitive and objective for monitoring the resolution of actinomycosis?
items.explanation: CT and MRI are generally the most sensitive and objective techniques for monitoring the resolution of actinomycosis.

items.question: For penicillin-allergic patients with actinomycosis, which alternative treatments are considered reasonable?
items.explanation: For penicillin-allergic patients, tetracyclines, ceftriaxone, or carbapenems are reasonable alternatives.

items.statement: Most actinomycotic infections are caused by a single pathogen.
items.explanation: Most if not all actinomycotic infections are polymicrobial.

items.statement: Genome-based analysis has led to the reclassification of some Actinomyces species into new genera.
items.explanation: Genome-based analysis and comparative 16S rRNA gene sequencing have led to the identification of an ever-expanding list of Actinomyces species and a reclassification of some species to other genera.

items.statement: Actinomycosis has a well-established association with SARS-CoV-2 infection.
items.explanation: The association of actinomycosis with SARS-CoV-2 infection is reported but not well-established.

items.statement: Actinomycosis spreads rapidly and invades tissue planes.
items.explanation: Actinomycosis spreads slowly, contiguously, and ignores tissue planes.

items.statement: Actinomyces can be detected in urine with appropriate stains and cultures.
items.explanation: Actinomyces can indeed be detected in urine using appropriate staining techniques and cultures.
Ascites is uncommon in pelvic actinomycosis, unlike malignancy and tuberculosis.

items.statement: Actinomycotic infection of bones and joints can result from hematogenous spread.
items.explanation: Actinomycotic infection of bones and joints may be associated with hematogenous spread.

items.statement: Actinomycetoma is most commonly caused by Actinomyces species.
items.explanation: Despite its name, Actinomycetoma is most commonly caused by Nocardia or Actinomadura species.

items.statement: Sulfur granules are a defining characteristic of actinomycosis.
items.explanation: Sulfur granules are indeed a defining characteristic of actinomycosis.

items.statement: Isolation of actinomycetes is maximized under aerobic conditions.
items.explanation: Isolation of actinomycetes is maximized under anaerobic conditions.

items.statement: Isolation of Actinomyces from blood cultures always necessitates treatment.
items.explanation: Isolation of Actinomyces from blood cultures in the absence of defined infection may represent contamination or transient bacteremia, and treatment may not be necessary.

items.statement: Surgical intervention is always required for actinomycosis.
items.explanation: An increasing body of literature supports an initial attempt at cure with medical therapy alone, even in extensive disease.

items.assertion: Actinomyces-like organisms have a high positive predictive value for diagnosing actinomycosis in women using an IUD.
items.reason: ALOs are identified in Pap-smear specimens in about 7% of women using an IUD.
items.explanation: The detection of ALOs has a low positive predictive value for diagnosing actinomycosis.

items.assertion: Thoracic actinomycosis is usually mistaken for neoplasm or pneumonia.
items.reason: Lesions suggestive of actinomycosis include those that cross fissures or pleura, extend into the mediastinum, contiguous bone, or chest wall.
items.explanation: The presence of these lesions leads to thoracic actinomycosis being mistaken for neoplasm or pneumonia.
